3_scrapingEvidence.description: Apify google-maps-scraper: 15 records. Wongnai via Browse AI: 10 records. Total: 25 records from 2 sources.

5_automationEvidence.description: n8n 4-node workflow: Daily Schedule Trigger → Score Restaurants (JS scoring logic) → Claude Haiku API (recommendation) → Build Final Report. Executed successfully in 1.295s.
5_automationEvidence.executionResult: Success in 1.295s — Claude responded: Gaggan Anor in Thonglor is an excellent choice for team dinners, offering innovative Indian cuisine in an elegant setting that accommodates groups well.
5_automationEvidence.executionScreenshot: Take screenshot of n8n canvas showing all 4 green nodes

6_top3Recommendation.name: The Commons Thonglor
6_top3Recommendation.reasoning: Best flexibility, value (300 THB/pp), and BTS access for mixed-preference teams

6_top3Recommendation.name: Ekkamai 63 Street Food
6_top3Recommendation.reasoning: Most budget-friendly (170 THB/pp), closest to BTS Ekkamai, authentic Thai experience

6_top3Recommendation.name: The Never Ending Summer
6_top3Recommendation.reasoning: Highest rating (4.7), iconic heritage venue, true group menu — best for special occasions

deliverables.7_reflection: Building this AI workflow taught me that data quality gates matter more than I expected. The biggest challenge was normalizing price strings — I had to build a robust parser before any scoring was meaningful. The scoring model's locked weights forced disciplined thinking: I could not adjust weights to favour a restaurant I personally preferred, which surfaced The Commons as the clear winner despite not being the fanciest option. The n8n automation was simpler than expected once the API calls were figured out. If I were to improve this workflow, I would add a second AI pass to cross-validate uniqueness scores against multiple review sources, and automate the HTML rebuild whenever new data arrives in the scoring sheet.
